TextENGNew: "...And that would be what, exactly?"
changeReason: Changes 'Who's that?' to 'And that would be what, exactly?'; JPN 何です asks 'what is it,' not 'who is that.'

TextENGNew: "Ridiculous! That's a worthless superstition!"
"Of course it's a superstition. Just a folk tale, nothing more."
"Worthless! ...You came all this way to cover something this worthless?!"
"...For someone who keeps calling it worthless, you do seem rather bothered by it."
"Arakawa-kun, settle down, settle down. I don't quite follow, but it's just a folk tale, isn't it?"
"...Yes. Just a folk tale."
"...What on earth are they talking about? ...Sonozaki-san, do you know? ...This whatever-sama business?"
"Well, we did learn about it in elementary school... ...So, for now—"
"Oyashiro-sama is the name of Hinamizawa's guardian deity. Everyone from around here knows it."
"I've had my fill of Oyashiro-sama stories! Are you really going to make me sit through more of that rubbish?!"
"...Stories?"
"...The folk tales about Oyashiro-sama. ...When someone tries to defile Hinamizawa..."
""...............there will be a curse.""
changeReason: Old translation 'Curse will...' is grammatically incomplete and unclear; new translation '...there will be a curse' provides a complete and accurate rendering of JPN 祟るの.

TextENGNew: Distant thunder.@k@rThe sound of rain grows a little louder.@k@rA brief silence.
"What nonsense! ...Every village in the country has a story or two like that!"
"Quite right. The legends of cursing gods are scattered all across Japan, more than you could count. ...But the curse of Oyashiro-sama is altogether unlike any of them."
"...He truly does curse people."
"......Ahaha, hahahahahaha! ...Oh come on! Curses went out of fashion ages ago! You're overdoing the spooky act, Rika-san."
"...It seems you don't know, Mion-chan. ...Well. ...That's understandable. Not everything made it into the papers."
"...Let me give you a concrete number. ...Four. ...Already four employees of the Ministry of Construction......... have died."
"In ways that were far from ordinary."
"Preposterous! Misfortune can coincide by chance! ...Listen to me! We are always involved in some dam project or another."
"I won't have people crying 'curse' every time someone collapses on duty!"
"If these had been ordinary deaths, I wouldn't be raising any such claims. ...But what happened to them was not ordinary."
"...Not ordinary...?"
"...Are you talking about Kondou-san... toward the end of last year...?"
"...That one made quite a splash in the papers."
"November 20th of last year. ...A horrifying incident—he attacked his sleeping family one by one, then cut his own throat and died."
"...The man's name was Kondou Yoshiyuki. ...A staffer in the Development Division of the Ministry of Construction's River Bureau."
"He was one of the original staff on the Hinamizawa dam project, and had visited Hinamizawa many times."
"...So it was a forced family suicide...?"
changeReason: Changes 'murder/suicide thing' to 'forced family suicide'; JPN 無理心中 specifically means a forced group/family suicide, a recognized Japanese concept.

TextENGNew: "In the end, yes. That was the conclusion. ...But no matter how deeply the police investigated, they couldn't find any reason he would have done it."
"...I remember rumors going around that he might have gotten into drugs."
"The autopsy found no trace of any substance whatsoever. His schedule at the time was brutally packed, so the police concluded it was a violent breakdown brought on by exhaustion."
"...I'll grant that it was a tragedy. And yes, it was abnormal! ...But leaping straight to Oyashiro-sama's curse is far too rash!"
"...Precisely. ...If this were an isolated incident, I wouldn't make that leap either."
"...There were others? ...Like that...?"
changeReason: Changes 'Who were they?' to 'Like that?'; JPN そーいうの means 'that kind of thing,' asking about similar incidents, not asking for names.

TextENGNew: "Come to think of it, Yoshimura-san, you said four staff members died. ...But I've heard nothing beyond the Kondou case."
"If a staff member had died, a notice would certainly have been circulated. ...Isn't that right, Arakawa-kun?!"
"...Forgive the question, but did you transfer to this section in April, Tonegawa-san?"
"Wh... yes! Until April I was in the Slope Conservation Section of the Erosion Control Department."
changeReason: Changes 'Land Conservation Division' to 'Slope Conservation Section'; JPN 傾斜地保全課 specifically means slope/hillside conservation, not general land conservation.

TextENGNew: "...It's being suppressed. ...There have been more deaths."
"Ah, Arakawa-kun?! Is that true?!"
Arakawa bows his head, face twisted in a grimace.@k@rTonegawa has no idea what is going on.
"In the wake of that incident, a certain rumor began to circulate among those handling the Hinamizawa dam."
"This rumor had been whispered even before the incident, but until that point it was dismissed as mere folklore."
"...Folklore, meaning... could it be—"
"Yes. ...Oyashiro-sama's... curse."
"Please stop!"
"...What kind of story is it, exactly? This... folk tale about Oyashiro-sama?"
"It's a pointless story!"
"...Long, long ago, there lived a poor old couple and their three sons."
"...The three sons despised fieldwork and longed for a life of luxury, so one day they abandoned their parents and the fields their ancestors had tended, and set off for the capital."
"All three of them fled at once, can you believe it. ...So the old man and his wife worked hard together to keep their ancestors' land going, but... it was hopeless."
"...In the end, one day they both collapsed and took to their beds."
"They needed their sons' strength to protect their ancestors' fields. ...So the old couple prayed to Oyashiro-sama. ...Please, they said, tell our sons to come home."
``...Oyashiro-sama went first to the eldest son, appearing to him in a dream and urging him to return home. ...But the eldest son, intoxicated by the city's bright life, flatly refused.``
changeReason: Old translates 夢枕に立って as 'stood at the son's bedside' — misses the dream/supernatural element. Japanese means 'appeared in a dream,' which is significant for Oyashiro-sama's nature.

TextENGNew: "Oyashiro-sama appeared to the eldest son in his dreams for seven nights. ...But the eldest son would not return to Hinamizawa."
"Having someone show up at your bedside every night sounds rough. ...That's practically harassment."
"...And then?"
"In the end, the eldest son is struck dead by the curse!"
"Next, Oyashiro-sama appeared to the second son in his dreams. ...But the second son would not return either."
"Same with the second! On the seventh day he loses his mind and dies!"
"Terrified by his brothers' strange deaths, the third son rushed home. ...And the three of them—father, mother, and son—worked together to keep the fields. ...The end."
changeReason: Clarifies that 'the three of them' means father, mother, and surviving son (not three brothers); two sons had died, so the JPN ３人 refers to the old couple plus the third son.

TextENGNew: "It's considered a folk tale meant to instill the custom of living and dying in the place of one's birth. ...Such customs are not so uncommon."
"...But Kondou-san wasn't from here to begin with, was he?"
"No, he wasn't. ...In other words, Oyashiro-sama is severe toward both those who try to leave Hinamizawa and those who come in from outside."
"...Being a guardian deity, you understand."
"Every last one of the old dam opponents trots this story out!"
"...We all found it rather charming. ...We never expected to hear folk tales like that in this day and age!"
"Yes. It was a folk tale. ...Until the incident occurred."
Thunder in the distance.@k@rThe rain continues to pour.@k@rA moment of silence.
"I don't remember who said it first. ...They were saying it had been rough not being able to sleep lately. ...That whenever they tried to sleep they'd feel restless and wake right up!"
"...Overwork."

"Then someone started saying it. When I try to sleep, I feel some kind of presence. I turn on the light and look around, but no one's there. ...Yet the moment I turn the light back off, I feel it again!"
"...Everyone thought it was funny and teased him, calling it Oyashiro-sama's curse. Said Oyashiro-sama was appearing to him in his dreams!"
"...Looking back, I think it was Kondou-san who first complained about not sleeping. ...Kondou-san... in the days before the incident, things were no longer something you could laugh off."
"...He kept saying 'it's there.'"
changeReason: Changes 'He swore something was there' to 'He kept saying "it's there"'; new preserves the direct speech and the specific JPN word いる (to exist/be present).

TextENGNew: "...Every night, whenever he tried to sleep... 'it's there,' he'd say!"
changeReason: Changes 'he was there' to 'it's there'; JPN いる is ambiguous and doesn't specify a gendered subject. The old translation assumes 'he' (Oyashiro-sama) while the original leaves the presence unidentified.

TextENGNew: "...'It's there'... ...Haha... I don't like the sound of that..."
changeReason: Changes 'No way' to 'I don't like the sound of that'; JPN ヤダなぁ expresses discomfort/distaste, not disbelief.

TextENGNew: "And then the incident happened. ...At that moment, the folk tale was no longer a thing to be laughed at."
"For a while after that, the office was like a haunted house!"
"'I felt a strange presence in the break room!' 'When I stayed late past midnight, someone was standing right behind me!' 'Something followed me onto the train!'"
"...Sheer absurdity doesn't even begin to cover it!"
"It isn't absurd at all. ...He is there. ...Oyashiro-sama."
changeReason: Changes 'He does exist' to 'He is there'; JPN いる (to be present/be there) implies a physical or spiritual presence in a specific location, connecting to the earlier discussion about feeling something 'there' at night.
